meta.title: Body response matrix for glaze families
meta.note: This matrix is a directional guide to help choose test paths. It does not replace body-specific fit testing and witness-cone confirmation.

bodies.label: Red earthenware
traits: high visual contrast for defect lines

bodies.label: Grogged sculpture body
traits: less ideal for thin transparent liners

responses.colorShift: strong warm cast under clear
responses.surfaceShift: clarity can look lower because body tone dominates
watchFor: crazing lines are obvious
durability claims are limited by body porosity

responses.colorShift: cleanest low-fire color host behavior
responses.surfaceShift: good brightness if melt is complete
watchFor: pinholes if burnout is incomplete

responses.colorShift: warmer and dimmer than intended
responses.surfaceShift: low-fire clear may underfit maturity expectations
watchFor: body and glaze temperature mismatch can invalidate tests

responses.colorShift: cleaner than iron stoneware but still outside low-fire target behavior
responses.surfaceShift: host may look underdeveloped
watchFor: use cone-6 families instead when practical

responses.colorShift: visually clean but often not fit-safe at low-fire chemistry
responses.surfaceShift: apparent clarity can hide stress
watchFor: delayed crazing after cooldown

responses.colorShift: body texture dominates clear read
responses.surfaceShift: roughness and pinholes are amplified
watchFor: clear liners rarely align with this body intent

responses.colorShift: unreliable for clean clear-host goals
responses.surfaceShift: decorative crackle tendencies dominate
watchFor: treat as decorative-only context

responses.colorShift: body warmth strongly tints clear
responses.surfaceShift: can appear more amber than expected
watchFor: temperature and body mismatch for true cone-6 intent

responses.colorShift: cleaner than red earthenware but still not primary target body
responses.surfaceShift: can look overmelted or incompatible depending on body line
watchFor: validate body maturity first

responses.colorShift: slight warmth on iron-bearing bodies
responses.surfaceShift: stable and practical with correct fit branch
watchFor: compare liner interior separately from exterior effects

responses.colorShift: clean and neutral host behavior
responses.surfaceShift: best balance of clarity and forgiveness
watchFor: still verify delayed crazing on rims

responses.colorShift: maximum color purity
responses.surfaceShift: fit stress appears faster than on stoneware
watchFor: base clear may craze even when it looks ideal

responses.colorShift: clear read is disrupted by body tooth
responses.surfaceShift: pinholes and uneven sheen become more likely
watchFor: use sculptural surface families when possible

responses.colorShift: not reliable for cone-6 clear role
responses.surfaceShift: thermal-shock context dominates result
watchFor: not a functional liner path

responses.colorShift: warm body overwhelms sensitive pink and maroon goals
responses.surfaceShift: host clarity remains but warm target colors flatten
watchFor: body selection can fail warm-color goal before host chemistry

responses.colorShift: better than red body but still outside preferred temperature/body match
responses.surfaceShift: decent color read with limited fit confidence
watchFor: confirm body maturity and fit rigorously

responses.colorShift: moderate warming on iron stoneware
responses.surfaceShift: usable but less color-clean than white bodies
watchFor: warm host benefit can be lost on dark body tone

responses.colorShift: strong warm-color performance
responses.surfaceShift: best practical balance for warm color hosts
watchFor: still compare against base clear for fit margin

responses.colorShift: highest warm-color purity
responses.surfaceShift: host appears clean but can reveal fit stress
watchFor: if fit fails, blend approach with lower-expansion clear branch

responses.colorShift: fine warm-color nuance is mostly lost
responses.surfaceShift: surface tooth reduces host clarity value
watchFor: not ideal for color-critical warm host work

responses.colorShift: warm-color host strategy does not transfer
responses.surfaceShift: decorative shock path dominates
watchFor: use raku-specific families instead

responses.colorShift: blue shifts toward muddy green-brown zones
responses.surfaceShift: cream breaks flatten and lose contrast
watchFor: body warmth can nullify floating effect

responses.colorShift: better contrast but still outside preferred body/firing match
responses.surfaceShift: reactive movement can overrun low-fire body assumptions
watchFor: run control and fit become unreliable

responses.colorShift: deeper storm blue with warmer breaks
responses.surfaceShift: strong break and pool behavior
watchFor: thickness and texture drive result more than recipe name

responses.colorShift: cleanest blue/cream separation for production-style tests
responses.surfaceShift: high visual range with manageable control
watchFor: confirm run distance before production use

responses.colorShift: very bright contrast and strong pool depth
responses.surfaceShift: reactivity can become excessive quickly
watchFor: small thickness changes can create large run changes

responses.colorShift: color complexity rises but clarity drops
responses.surfaceShift: texture-driven break can be dramatic but rough
watchFor: body tooth and glaze run can combine into instability

responses.colorShift: unreliable transfer of cone-6 floating behavior
responses.surfaceShift: thermal-shock process dominates
watchFor: treat as separate decorative test path

responses.colorShift: matte warms quickly and can look muddy
responses.surfaceShift: dryness risk rises
watchFor: functional matte goals usually mismatch low-fire porous bodies

responses.colorShift: softer pastel read
responses.surfaceShift: can appear chalky if under-melted
watchFor: cone and body mismatch before chemistry

responses.colorShift: warmer matte language
responses.surfaceShift: good matte depth with controlled cooling
watchFor: cutlery marking and cleanability checks are mandatory

responses.colorShift: clean matte and satin reads
responses.surfaceShift: most reliable body for matte branch comparisons
watchFor: cooling profile can shift sheen more than expected

responses.colorShift: cooler, cleaner matte tones
responses.surfaceShift: fit stress and dryness appear early on tight body
watchFor: use lower-thickness tests before full-coverage application

responses.colorShift: matte can accent body roughness strongly
responses.surfaceShift: dryness and pinholes are more likely
watchFor: prefer sculptural surface families for coarse bodies

responses.colorShift: functional matte framework does not map
responses.surfaceShift: decorative response dominates
watchFor: not a functional matte path

responses.colorShift: amber collapses toward darker brown
responses.surfaceShift: natural transitions become heavy
watchFor: too much warmth can erase family nuance

responses.colorShift: warmer amber still visible
responses.surfaceShift: branch differences are visible but body fit remains weak at target cone
watchFor: evaluate as aesthetic test, not production fit conclusion

responses.colorShift: rich amber and rutile-brown transitions
responses.surfaceShift: strong natural movement with moderate control
watchFor: raw-slip drying behavior can confuse chemistry interpretation

responses.colorShift: cleanest reading of family transitions
responses.surfaceShift: best platform for branch comparison
watchFor: branch intent can diverge quickly with thickness

responses.colorShift: lighter and sometimes unexpectedly bright transitions
responses.surfaceShift: reactive branches can run more dramatically
watchFor: use conservative thickness and catch support

responses.colorShift: earthier and rougher natural look
responses.surfaceShift: texture emphasizes natural branch character
watchFor: surface roughness may exceed functional tolerance

responses.colorShift: family does not map cleanly to raku process
responses.surfaceShift: thermal-shock route overrides intended cone-6 behavior
watchFor: treat as separate decorative experimentation

responses.colorShift: body/firing mismatch invalidates high-fire clear intent
responses.surfaceShift: not a practical use path
watchFor: do not generalize high-fire clear behavior to low-fire bodies

responses.colorShift: outside normal body range
responses.surfaceShift: non-representative result
watchFor: body suitability fails first

responses.colorShift: slight warmth on iron bodies
responses.surfaceShift: reliable clear and liner behavior with proper maturity
watchFor: atmosphere and reduction control become major variables

responses.colorShift: clean transparent read
responses.surfaceShift: strong functional-candidate platform
watchFor: verify cone placement across kiln zones

responses.colorShift: maximum clarity and pool visibility
responses.surfaceShift: fit and run shifts are highly visible
watchFor: porcelain exposes fit stress quickly

responses.colorShift: texture softens clarity value
responses.surfaceShift: transparent role may be less useful than atmospheric families
watchFor: evaluate if clear intent matches sculptural surface goals

responses.colorShift: high-fire clear model does not transfer
responses.surfaceShift: decorative process mismatch
watchFor: keep raku and high-fire functional paths separate

responses.colorShift: raku effects are unstable and low-repeatability on non-raku bodies
responses.surfaceShift: thermal-shock mismatch can cause failures
watchFor: use raku clay for process validity

responses.colorShift: decorative effects appear but reliability is poor
responses.surfaceShift: crackle and carbon effects are unpredictable
watchFor: body thermal-shock behavior is limiting factor

responses.colorShift: raku effects possible but body shock tolerance varies
responses.surfaceShift: risk of cracking increases with dense stoneware
watchFor: test small pieces before larger forms

responses.colorShift: can show bright decorative effects with careful process
responses.surfaceShift: still less shock-safe than true raku body
watchFor: decorative-only use remains likely

responses.colorShift: high crack risk and low process reliability
responses.surfaceShift: not a stable raku substrate
watchFor: avoid unless specifically researching failure envelope

responses.colorShift: decorative effects can be strong with coarse texture
responses.surfaceShift: surface ruggedness increases
watchFor: test reduction timing consistency

responses.colorShift: intended metallic, crackle, and smoke effects
responses.surfaceShift: best shock resilience for raku process
watchFor: maintain decorative-only functional status unless proven otherwise

decisionRules.rule: Pick body and firing context first, then select host or effect branch.
decisionRules.why: Many color and fit failures are body-context failures, not recipe failures.

decisionRules.rule: Use a white or light body for color-host and stain-host decisions before validating on warmer bodies.
decisionRules.why: Body color can hide or fake host chemistry performance.

decisionRules.rule: Keep decorative branches and functional-candidate branches in separate test tracks.
decisionRules.why: Reactive and raku surface success does not imply functional durability.
